terms.definition: Practice of emitting structured records of program activity (with severity, timestamp, context) for after-the-fact investigation and monitoring.
A named entry point (`logging.getLogger('ragapp.retrieval')`) that fires log records; organised hierarchically by dot-name.
Severity tier: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL; chosen deliberately to control signal/noise.
Component that takes a log record and routes it to a destination (console, file, syslog, Sentry); a logger may have many.
Component that turns a log record into a string (plain text, JSON, etc.) for a handler to write.
Component attached to a logger or handler that decides whether each record passes through; used for redaction and conditional emission.
Logs emitted as parseable key/value records (typically JSON) rather than free text; required for serious log-store querying.
A per-request identifier (request_id, trace_id) propagated through every log line so concurrent requests can be reassembled.
Standard Python idiom for configuring logging from a dictionary (loggers, handlers, formatters), usually loaded once at application start.
Popular Python library for structured logging with context binding (`bind_contextvars`) and JSON rendering on top of `logging`.
Built-in handler that discards all records; added to library loggers to suppress the 'no handler' warning when no app config is loaded.
